description:
You are acting as the on-call backup administrator.  
A small data set that must be archived and registered with the remote backup-tracking API has been staged for you under:

/home/user/data/file_a.txt  
/home/user/data/file_b.txt

Goal  
1. Produce a compressed archive of *everything* that is located inside /home/user/data and store it in a brand-new directory called /home/user/backups.  
   • The archive file **must** be named exactly “backup-latest.tar.gz”.  
2. Create a JSON manifest that describes the archive you just produced.  
   • Save the manifest as /home/user/backups/backup_manifest.json.  
   • The file has to be valid JSON (no trailing commas) and use this precise, three-field structure and order:

{
  "filename": "backup-latest.tar.gz",
  "size_bytes": <integer number of bytes of the archive>,
  "sha256": "<64-character lowercase hex digest of the archive>"
}

   • Make sure size_bytes is an integer, *not* a string.  
   • The sha256 value must match the actual SHA-256 digest of /home/user/backups/backup-latest.tar.gz.  
3. Use curl to POST the manifest to the public echo endpoint https://httpbin.org/post.  
   • Save the complete body that the server returns to /home/user/backups/api_response.json.  
   • While doing the POST, also capture the HTTP status code.  
4. Create a single-line log file named /home/user/backups/api_test.log that records the result of the API call in this **exact** format (no extra whitespace):

POST https://httpbin.org/post -> 200

Everything under /home/user/backups must be writable by your regular user.  
No other output files should be created anywhere on the filesystem.

The automated checker will verify the following:

• /home/user/backups/backup-latest.tar.gz exists and, when extracted, yields the same two files placed under /home/user/data with their original contents.  
• /home/user/backups/backup_manifest.json is valid JSON, its “filename” field is backup-latest.tar.gz, and the size_bytes and sha256 fields correctly describe the archive.  
• /home/user/backups/api_response.json contains the JSON object echoed back by https://httpbin.org/post, i.e. the object found under the “json” key inside the response must exactly match the contents of backup_manifest.json.  
• /home/user/backups/api_test.log exists and **matches the line shown above verbatim**.

Important reminders  
• Do not hard-code any numbers; compute the archive size and SHA-256 digest programmatically so the manifest remains correct even if the data changes.  
• Only the four files mentioned above should be written.  
• Use any combination of typical GNU/Linux user-land tools (tar, sha256sum, stat, curl, jq, etc.) to accomplish the task, but keep the sequence to a simple 3–4 command workflow.  
• Your solution will run without elevated (root) privileges, so stay within /home/user.  
• The checker will start with a pristine /home/user/backups directory that does **not** exist yet.
truth:
Initial files that already exist before the agent starts:

/home/user/data/file_a.txt  
Content (including final newline):
Alpha
(size: 6 bytes)

/home/user/data/file_b.txt  
Content (including final newline):
Beta
(size: 5 bytes)

Expected agent-produced artefacts:

1. /home/user/backups/backup-latest.tar.gz  
   • Must be a gzip-compressed tar archive that contains the two files above inside the archive’s root (i.e., ./file_a.txt and ./file_b.txt).  
   • The files’ contents inside the archive must match exactly.

2. /home/user/backups/backup_manifest.json  
   • Valid JSON.  
   • Three keys in the exact order: filename, size_bytes, sha256.  
   • filename == "backup-latest.tar.gz"  
   • size_bytes == size of /home/user/backups/backup-latest.tar.gz in bytes (will be programmatically recomputed by the checker).  
   • sha256 == 64-char hex lowercase digest of the archive (recomputed by the checker).

3. /home/user/backups/api_response.json  
   • Valid JSON returned by https://httpbin.org/post.  
   • Its "json" sub-object must be byte-for-byte identical to the contents of backup_manifest.json.

4. /home/user/backups/api_test.log  
   • Single line, exact content:
POST https://httpbin.org/post -> 200
(no trailing or preceding whitespace, ends with a single newline)

The checker will:

A. Confirm the /home/user/backups directory exists and holds exactly the four files named above (no extras).  
B. Extract backup-latest.tar.gz into a temporary location and verify file_a.txt and file_b.txt are present and their checksums match the originals.  
C. Parse backup_manifest.json and verify the size_bytes and sha256 fields match the real archive.  
D. Load api_response.json, locate the "json" field echoed by httpbin, and compare it with the manifest for equality.  
E. Check api_test.log for the single, exact line described.
